data.title: Software stock multiples continue to drop, even those making double-digit gains: RBC
data.description:
A wide array of software stocks continues to tumble in 2026, possibly creating a plethora of attractive entry points for investors, as RBC attempts to calculate where the bottom might be in the recent multiple compression.

Fears of artificial intelligence disruption have severely weighed down the software sector. The iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) has already shed more than 20% year to date.

"To level set, while we understand concerns around stock-based compensation and believe it is a real expense, we believe the GAAP P&L is not the best tool for SaaS companies, given the mismatch between revenue (which is ratably recognized) and expenses (mostly recognized upfront)," said RBC analysts, led by Rishi Jaluria, in an investor note. "Having said that, given ongoing terminal value concerns in software, we believe GAAP earnings could start to build a floor on many software names."

RBC compiled a list of enterprise software names trading below the S&P 500 (SP500) multiple of 19x 2027 P/E. This includes Adobe (ADBE) at 12.5x, Salesforce (CRM) at 18x, NICE (NICE) at 13x, PagerDuty (PD) at 14x, and Qualys (QYLS) at 17x. What's more, double-digit growth names such as Microsoft (MSFT) and Workday (WDAY) are only trading at 20x and 21x, respectively.

Enterprise software companies that have worked exhaustively to produce AI tools and have partnered with premier AI companies are far from immune. Even when those endeavors are generating double-digit growth.

Shannon Saccocia, chief investment officer at Neuberger Berman Private Wealth, argued that recent market selling of AI-adjacent companies has been overly broad and reactive.

"This indiscriminate selling really offers up opportunities for active management where you're selecting companies that do have that competitive advantage that actually can take AI and make it work," she said.

Earlier this week, Marko Kolanovic, former chief strategist and co-head of Global Research at J.P. Morgan, opined that maybe software companies should try to distance themselves from AI to stop the bleeding.

"To rally, ironically, the market will need a hyperscaler or a software company to say they will stop investing in AI (stop buying inflated memory) and refocus on cash flow," Kolanovic said in a post on X.

data.title: Humana buys Florida-based primary care organization MaxHealth
data.description:
• Humana’s (HUM) healthcare services unit, CenterWell, has acquired MaxHealth, a Florida-based primary care organization serving more than 120,000 patients through a network of 82 healthcare clinics spread across the western and southern parts of the state.
  • The company has acquired the clinic operator from Arsenal Capital Partners, a New York-based private equity firm, and MaxHealth’s founder-shareholders, CenterWell said in a press release on Friday.
  • The Tampa-based firm operates 54 primary care clinics, four specialty/ancillary clinics, and 24 affiliate clinics in West and South Florida. Its customer base includes over 80,000 patients in value-based care programs.
  • The financial terms of the transaction were not disclosed. On Thursday, Bloomberg reported that Humana (HUM) was nearing a deal worth nearly $1B to acquire MaxHealth from Arsenal Capital Partners.

data.title: Casino meltdown: Will gold-to-cash machines move in next to ATMs?
data.description:
The big rally in gold prices has generated interest in China in machines that can melt down the precious metal and disperse cash.

Gold ATMs can melt or otherwise process small items of gold on site, assess purity and weight with sensors, quote a dynamic price linked to spot markets, and then pay out electronically or in cash. The machines are an obvious way to displace traditional pawnshops and street gold buyers by emphasizing speed, transparency, and lower labor costs while capturing the spread between the buy price and wholesale resale. Notably, the units function as a robotic front end to an integrated refining and recycling operation, with economics driven by transaction volume, metal recovery yields, and margin management relative to spot gold.

The concept is most advanced in China, where machines have appeared in major cities such as Shanghai and Beijing within high-traffic malls, metro hubs, and financial districts. Operators position them as a response to surging gold prices and consumer demand for liquidity without paperwork or lengthy verification. Several Chinese and Hong Kong–based technology and metals firms are also testing them as part of broader gold recycling and refining businesses.

In the casino sector, the gold-melting ATM concept is also being used at the L'Arc Macao hotel in Macau and reportedly is being considered by other Asian casino operators. While the technology presents a compelling deployment opportunity in or near casinos in the U.S., regulatory barriers in Nevada and other key gaming jurisdictions could stand in the way. Casinos in the U.S. are required to be sensitive to anything touching money laundering, stolen property, and consumer protection.

data.title: Valero to import up to 6.5M barrels of Venezuelan crude in March - Reuters
data.description:
Valero Energy (VLO) is poised to buy as many as 6.5M barrels of Venezuelan crude in March bound for its Gulf Coast refineries, which could make it the top foreign refiner of Venezuelan oil since the U.S. captured President Maduro in January, Reuters reported Friday.

If Valero (VLO) succeeds in acquiring 10 or more cargoes next month, equivalent to ~210K bbl/day, the company could surpass Chevron (CVX) as the top U.S. refiner of Venezuelan crude, at least for now.

Chevron (CVX), the only U.S. major producing oil in Venezuela, is expected to raise exports of Venezuelan crude to ~300K bbl/day in March from 220K bbl/day in January, sources told Reuters last month.

Chevron (CVX) typically refines up to half of those exports at its own refineries and sells the rest to other U.S. refiners; much of Chevron's sales of Venezuelan oil to U.S. refiners typically go to Valero (VLO), and Chevron is expected to supply Valero with most of the volume the refiner is planning to import in March, according to the report.

Valero (VLO), which has the second-largest U.S. refining network capable of processing Venezuelan heavy oil, had a long-term supply agreement to buy crude from the country before the U.S. first sanctioned the country's oil industry in January 2019.

Valero's (VLO) total refining capacity for Venezuelan crude oil totaled ~240K bbl/day before a 2023 expansion at its 435K bbl/day refinery in Port Arthur, Texas, and the company has said it is now capable of processing a much larger volume of Venezuelan oil.

data.title: Exxon can sue California AG for defamation over recycling, judge rules
data.description:
A U.S. district judge on Friday rejected California Attorney General Rob Bonta's request to dismiss Exxon Mobil's (XOM) lawsuit accusing him of defamation in criticizing the company's initiatives around advanced plastics recycling, Reuters reported.

The judge rebuffed Bonta's claim that he was immune from being sued because he criticized Exxon (XOM) in his official capacity and in the "heartland" of his employment duties.

The judge also said whether the AG criticized Exxon (XOM) in good faith was a question of fact to be determined later.

Exxon (XOM) sued Bonta in January 2025, after California had filed a lawsuit accusing the company of engaging in decades of lies about the limitations of recycling.

The company has shown more willingness to fight back against environmentalists and other critics who say its operations harm the climate and add to greenhouse gas emissions.
